section.title: The Ransomed Shall Return
section.sectionRationale: This short, luminous chapter closes the seam of the final woes and the ransomed return, chapters 33 to 35, answering the desolation of Edom in chapter 34 with a vision of the wilderness itself blossoming, the blind and lame healed, and the ransomed of the LORD walking a highway of holiness home to Zion with everlasting joy.
section.thesis: The same LORD who devotes His enemies to total desolation will just as surely transform the wilderness into a garden, heal the blind and the lame, and bring His ransomed people home along a Way of Holiness, crowned forever with joy that sorrow can never again touch.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the wilderness bursting into bloom; strengthening the weak and fearful; eyes opened and ears unstopped; the highway called the Way of Holiness; and the climactic return of the ransomed to Zion with everlasting joy.

units.label: The wilderness will blossom
units.note: the glory of the LORD revealed

verses.text: The wilderness and the land will be glad; the desert will rejoice and blossom like a rose.
It will bloom profusely and rejoice with joy and singing. The glory of Lebanon will be given to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon. They will see the glory of the LORD, the splendor of our God.
Strengthen the limp hands and steady the feeble knees!
Say to those with anxious hearts: “Be strong, do not fear! Behold, your God will come with vengeance. With divine retribution He will come to save you.”

units.label: Eyes opened, ears unstopped
units.note: the blind, the lame, the mute

verses.text: Then the eyes of the blind will be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped.
Then the lame will leap like a deer and the mute tongue will shout for joy. For waters will gush forth in the wilderness, and streams in the desert.
The parched ground will become a pool, the thirsty land springs of water. In the haunt where jackals once lay, there will be grass and reeds and papyrus.
And there will be a highway called the Way of {{qodesh|Holiness}}. The unclean will not travel it, only those who walk in the Way, and fools will not stray onto it.
No lion will be there, and no vicious beast will go up on it. Such will not be found there, but the redeemed will walk upon it.
So the {{padah|redeemed}} of the LORD will return and enter Zion with singing, crowned with everlasting joy. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee.

qodesh.gloss: Holiness, set-apartness. The highway home is named the Way of Holiness because it is reserved for a purified people; the unclean cannot travel it. It anticipates Christ, who does not merely mark the road but calls Himself the Way, John 14:6, the one path by which the unclean are made holy and brought home.

padah.gloss: To redeem or ransom, to free someone by paying a price on their behalf. The redeemed of the LORD are those He Himself has purchased and set free, language that runs through Isaiah's whole Book of Comfort and finds its costliest fulfillment in Christ, who ransoms His people not with silver but with His own blood, 1 Peter 1:18-19.

basic.context: After Edom's desolation in chapter 34, this short chapter turns to glorious hope: the desert will bloom, weak hands and feeble knees will be strengthened, the blind will see and the lame will leap, and a highway called the Way of Holiness will carry the ransomed of the LORD home to Zion with everlasting joy. It is one of the most beautiful visions of restoration in the whole Old Testament.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter first spoke hope to exiles longing to return home, picturing their journey transformed by God's presence, water in the desert, a safe highway, no danger from beasts. Jesus Himself later applies this very language, blind eyes opened, the lame leaping, to His own healing ministry, Matthew 11:4-5, showing that the return from exile was always meant to point toward something greater: the Messiah's own work of healing and restoration.
basic.originalLanguages: The highway is named the Way of {{qodesh}}, holiness, reserved for a purified people alone. Those who walk it are the {{padah}}, the redeemed or ransomed, people bought back at a price. Together the images describe not just a road home but a people made fit to walk it, ransomed and set apart, entering Zion with singing.
basic.typology: The healing of the blind, deaf, lame, and mute anticipates Christ's own miracles, which He explicitly points to as proof of His identity, Matthew 11:4-5. The Way of Holiness anticipates Christ, who calls Himself the Way, the one path to the Father, John 14:6. The everlasting joy that finally banishes sorrow and sighing anticipates the new creation, where God wipes away every tear, Revelation 21:4.
basic.christ: When John the Baptist's disciples ask if Jesus is the Coming One, Jesus answers by pointing to exactly this chapter's signs fulfilled: the blind see, the lame walk, Matthew 11:4-5. Jesus is Himself the Way of Holiness, John 14:6, the one path by which the unclean are cleansed and brought home to God. The everlasting joy promised here, with sorrow and sighing forever fled, is the same joy Revelation 21:4 promises when Christ makes all things new.
basic.god: God is the one who transforms wilderness into blossom and turns weak, fearful people into a strong, singing procession. He comes not merely to punish but to save, opening blind eyes and lame legs as signs of His own presence among His people, and He guarantees that those He has ransomed will reach home, crowned with joy that sorrow can never again touch.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 11:4-5 shows Jesus citing this very chapter's signs as proof of His messianic identity. John 14:6 identifies Jesus as the Way. Revelation 21:4 promises the final wiping away of every tear and the end of sorrow. Isaiah 40:3-5, the next movement, develops the same highway image for the LORD's coming.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's vision of a blooming desert, healed bodies, and a holy highway home is one of Scripture's clearest windows into the whole Bible's hope: God ransoming a broken, exiled people and bringing them home with joy. Jesus fulfills its signs in His own healing ministry and is Himself the Way of Holiness, and its promise of everlasting joy awaits its full completion in the new creation.

context.literary: A tightly structured hymn of hope in five movements, moving from the transformed wilderness, through encouragement to the weak and fearful, to healing of the blind and lame, to the highway home, and closing with the ransomed entering Zion with singing.
context.historical: Addressed most immediately to exiles who would need real hope for an actual journey home from Babylon, promising water, safety from wild beasts, and a clear path, while its language consistently exceeds a merely logistical description of a road trip.
context.canonical: Forms a matching pair with Isaiah 34's judgment on Edom, closing this seam of the book, and anticipates the highway and comfort language that opens the Book of Comfort in chapter 40, as well as the healing signs Jesus points to as evidence of His messianic mission.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort a people facing exile and loss with the certain hope that God will not only bring them home but transform the very conditions of their journey and their bodies, healing what is broken and turning sorrow into everlasting joy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's promised, certain restoration; it is not a prescription for how believers should engineer their own comfort or healing. Its abiding truth is that God ransoms His people and that ultimate healing and homecoming are found in Him, supremely fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Are the healings of the blind, deaf, lame, and mute in verses 5 to 6 meant literally, or are they figures for the transformed condition of the returning exiles?

views.label: Figurative language for a transformed journey and nation
views.case: In its first setting, the imagery poetically describes a people so renewed by God's saving presence that even their weakness and disability seem to vanish on the road home, matching the wider poetic transformation of the desert itself.

views.label: A genuine prophecy of literal healing, fulfilled in Christ
views.case: Jesus applies this very language to His own literal miracles of healing blind eyes and lame legs, Matthew 11:4-5, treating the prophecy as finding real, bodily fulfillment in His ministry, not merely a poetic figure.

debates.bearing: The two readings need not compete: the prophecy comforted returning exiles with vivid imagery of transformation, and Jesus shows that its truest and fullest meaning is realized in His own literal, bodily healing ministry as Messiah.

study.originalLanguages: The highway home is the Way of {{qodesh}}, holiness, a road reserved for a people set apart, and those who walk it are the {{padah}}, the redeemed, ransomed at a price rather than merely released. Together these words describe a homecoming that is not automatic but purchased and purified, exactly the pattern the New Testament sees completed in Christ.

typology.type: The blind seeing, the lame leaping, the mute singing
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' own healing miracles, which He cites as proof of His messianic identity, Matthew 11:4-5.

typology.type: The Way of Holiness, a road only the redeemed can walk
typology.fulfillment: Christ Himself, who declares, 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life,' John 14:6.

typology.type: Everlasting joy with sorrow and sighing forever fled
typology.fulfillment: The new creation, where God wipes away every tear and there is no more mourning, Revelation 21:4.

study.christ: When John the Baptist sends messengers asking whether Jesus is the one to come, Jesus answers by pointing directly to this chapter's signs already happening in His ministry: the blind see, the lame walk, the deaf hear, Matthew 11:4-5. Jesus does not merely walk the Way of Holiness, He is that Way, John 14:6, the one path by which the unclean are made fit to enter Zion. The everlasting joy promised at this chapter's climax, with sorrow and sighing forever fled, is the same joy Christ secures and Revelation 21:4 promises will be completed when He makes all things new.
study.god: God is the one who turns desolate wilderness into blossom and turns a weak, fearful, broken people into a healed, singing procession home. He comes with vengeance against His enemies and salvation for His people in the very same act, and He ensures that everyone He has ransomed will actually arrive, crowned with a joy that sorrow can never again interrupt.

crossReferences.note: Jesus cites the healing of the blind and the lame as evidence He is the promised Messiah, directly echoing this chapter.
Jesus declares Himself the Way, fulfilling the image of the Way of Holiness that only the redeemed can walk.
God wipes away every tear and ends all mourning, the completion of this chapter's promise that sorrow and sighing will flee.
Develops the same highway imagery for the LORD's coming, opening the Book of Comfort that follows this seam.

study.oneStory: This chapter's vision of a blossoming desert, healed bodies, and a holy highway home to Zion is a window into the whole Bible's hope: a God who ransoms a broken people and brings them home with everlasting joy. Christ fulfills its healing signs, is Himself the Way of Holiness, and guarantees the final joy this chapter promises will be completed forever in the new creation.

quiz.q: What transformation does the wilderness undergo in verses 1 to 2?

options.text: It becomes glad and blossoms like a rose, sharing in the glory of Lebanon, Carmel, and Sharon.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 describe exactly this transformation of the wilderness into abundant, glorious bloom.

options.text: It remains barren but becomes safer to travel through.
options.note: The passage describes active flourishing and blossoming, not mere safety amid continued barrenness.

options.text: It is destroyed entirely and replaced by ocean.
options.note: The opposite; the wilderness blossoms and rejoices rather than being destroyed.

options.text: It becomes a permanent desert with no vegetation at all.
options.note: The text describes the desert rejoicing and blooming profusely, the reverse of permanent barrenness.

quiz.q: What signs of healing are promised in verses 5 to 6?

options.text: The blind will see, the deaf will hear, the lame will leap, and the mute will sing for joy.
options.note: These verses list exactly this set of healings, later cited by Jesus as messianic signs.

options.text: Only emotional comfort is promised, with no mention of physical healing.
options.note: The text specifically names physical conditions: blindness, deafness, lameness, and muteness.

options.text: Healing is promised only for children, not adults.
options.note: No such age restriction appears in the text; the healing is described generally.

options.text: The healings are limited to a select few religious leaders.
options.note: The text describes these signs as characterizing the whole restored community, not a select religious class.

quiz.q: Who may travel the highway called the Way of Holiness, according to verse 8?

options.text: Only those who walk in the Way; the unclean and fools will not travel it.
options.note: Verse 8 states this restriction directly, reserving the highway for the redeemed alone.

options.text: Anyone at all, regardless of moral or spiritual condition.
options.note: The verse specifically excludes the unclean and fools from this highway.

options.text: Only the wealthy elite of Jerusalem.
options.note: The qualification given is holiness and cleanness, not wealth or social status.

options.text: Only trained travelers with special equipment.
options.note: The qualification is moral and spiritual purity, not practical travel skill.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use the healing imagery of this chapter in the Gospels?

options.text: He points to the blind seeing and the lame walking as proof He is the promised Messiah, in answer to John the Baptist.
options.note: Matthew 11:4-5 records Jesus doing exactly this, directly echoing Isaiah 35.

options.text: He rejects this chapter as irrelevant to His ministry.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus directly applies this chapter's language to His own works.

options.text: He applies it only to a future, entirely different prophet.
options.note: Jesus applies it to His own present ministry, not to another future figure.

options.text: He uses it only as a metaphor with no connection to actual miracles.
options.note: Jesus points to His literal healing miracles as the fulfillment, not merely a metaphor.

quiz.q: What is promised about sorrow in the chapter's closing verse, verse 10?

options.text: Gladness and joy will overtake the ransomed, and sorrow and sighing will flee.
options.note: Verse 10 gives this exact promise as the climax of the chapter.

options.text: Sorrow will increase even as the people return home.
options.note: The opposite; sorrow and sighing are promised to flee, not increase.

options.text: Joy and sorrow will remain permanently mixed together.
options.note: The verse promises sorrow's flight, not a permanent mixture with joy.

options.text: Only some of the ransomed will experience this joy.
options.note: The promise is given to the whole company of the ransomed entering Zion, not a select few.

questions: The desert is promised to blossom and rejoice. Where in your life feels like a wilderness right now, and how does this promise shape your hope for it?
Verse 3 calls for strengthening limp hands and feeble knees. Who around you needs that kind of encouragement, and what would it look like for you to offer it this week?
Jesus pointed to blind eyes opened and lame legs healed as proof of who He is. Where do you need fresh confidence that Jesus is exactly who He claims to be?
Only the redeemed can walk the Way of Holiness. What does it mean for you personally that you walk this way not by your own effort but because you have been ransomed by Christ?
The chapter promises that sorrow and sighing will flee before everlasting joy. What sorrow are you carrying that you need to entrust to the God who promises this final joy?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints em dashes in this verse, they have been rendered as commas per house style.
This healing language is directly cited by Jesus in Matthew 11:4-5 as evidence of His messianic identity, making this one of Isaiah's clearest advance windows into the Gospel accounts.
This verse recurs almost verbatim at Isaiah 51:11, showing how central this image of joyful, sorrow-free homecoming is to the book's larger hope.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 35:1-10. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
